title: Sigara böreği
subtitle: Crisp filo cigars filled with white cheese and parsley
excerpt: Filo pastry strips filled with crumbled feta, egg, and parsley, rolled into tight cigars and fried until shatteringly crisp. The filling is made in seconds; rolling is meditative once you get the motion. Serve hot, immediately, as a starter or snack.
sourceNotes: Sigara böreği (the name means cigarette pastry) is a widespread Turkish and Balkan preparation documented in Ottoman and Turkish culinary literature from the nineteenth century. Börek as a category (filo or yufka pastry with various fillings) is one of the defining preparations of Ottoman palace and household cooking. White cheese (beyaz peynir, similar to feta) and parsley is the most common filling for this form. Similar preparations appear throughout the former Ottoman territories in Southeastern Europe and the Levant.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze uncooked on a tray until solid, then bag. Fry from frozen, adding 1 to 2 minutes. Do not thaw first.
recipe.batchNotes: Double or triple the recipe and freeze the extra uncooked.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Can be rolled and refrigerated, covered, for up to 4 hours before frying.

content.text: Sigara böreği is the easiest member of the börek family. No baking, no layering, no heavy pan: just a simple cheese filling rolled into a cigar shape and fried until the outside crackles. The key is sealing the tip properly — if the end is not pinched closed, the filling seeps into the oil. Egg white on the tip works better than water.

Filo sheets dry out fast. Keep them covered with a damp cloth while you work. The filling is a ratio rather than a fixed recipe: crumbled white cheese and egg, with parsley to cut the richness. Feta is readily available and the right saltiness; fresh white cheese (lor peyniri, ricotta) can be added to soften the flavour if the feta is very sharp.

items.prepNote: about 8 sheets; keep covered with a damp cloth
crumbled; or use half feta, half ricotta for a milder filling
1 beaten into the filling, 1 for sealing the tips
taste first; feta is already salty

content.text: Mash the {{feta}} with a fork until crumbly. Add one beaten {{eggs}} egg and the {{parsley-flat}} of parsley. Mix to combine. Taste — feta is salty; you likely need no additional salt. The filling should be cohesive but not wet.

Cut the {{filo-pastry}} sheets into strips about 10 cm wide and 25 cm long. Keep covered. Take one strip and place a heaped teaspoon of filling at the short end nearest you, leaving a 1 cm border on the sides. Fold the border edges in over the filling, then roll away from you tightly to form a cigar about 10 cm long. Dab the tip with the second beaten egg to seal firmly. Repeat with the remaining filo and filling.

Pour the {{vegetable-oil}} of oil into a frying pan to a depth of 2 cm. Heat over medium-high heat until a small piece of filo dropped in sizzles immediately. Fry the cigars in batches, seam-side down first, turning once, for 2 to 3 minutes total until evenly golden and crisp. Do not crowd the pan. Drain briefly on kitchen paper and serve immediately.

items.symptom: Filling leaks into the oil during frying
items.cause: Tip not sealed firmly; or rolled too loosely with gaps
items.fix: Dab a generous amount of beaten egg on the tip and press firmly; roll tightly without air pockets

items.symptom: Filo cracks and splits while rolling
items.cause: Filo dried out; not kept covered with a damp cloth
items.fix: Cover unused filo with a damp (not wet) cloth; work quickly; dried filo cannot be salvaged

items.symptom: Cigars are pale and greasy rather than golden and crisp
items.cause: Oil not hot enough; or too many cigars in the pan at once
items.fix: Heat oil until a piece of filo sizzles immediately on contact; fry in batches of four to six

items.cause: Feta used was very mild; parsley omitted
items.fix: Use block feta rather than pre-crumbled; include the parsley; taste the raw filling and add black pepper if needed

content.text: Börek (pastry made with thin layers of yufka or filo, filled with cheese, meat, or vegetables) is one of the foundational preparations of Ottoman palace cooking and spread with the Ottoman empire across Southeastern Europe, the Levant, and North Africa. Each territory adapted it to local fillings and pastry traditions: the Moroccan bastilla, the Greek spanakopita, and the Balkan burek all descend from the same Ottoman preparation. Sigara böreği is the simplest form: a single layer of filo around a modest filling, quick to make, quick to eat, and rarely lasting long enough at the table for anyone to feel they have had enough.
